items.question_text: A flight progress strip uses the clearance abbreviation "PD" from TBL 2-3-11. What clearance condition is being recorded?
items.explanation: TBL 2-3-11 defines PD as cleared to climb or descend at pilot's discretion.

choices.text: Cleared to climb or descend at pilot's discretion.

items.question_text: Using TBL 2-3-11, which meaning matches the clearance abbreviation "X" on a strip?
items.explanation: TBL 2-3-11 uses X for a clearance to cross an airway, route, or radial at a point.

choices.text: Cleared through for landing and takeoff.
Cleared to cross an airway, route, or radial at a point.

items.question_text: A strip annotation uses "RH" from TBL 2-3-12. What instruction does that abbreviation represent?
items.explanation: TBL 2-3-12 defines RH as runway heading.

items.question_text: A pilot reports a TCAS event. Which TBL 2-3-12 abbreviation corresponds to that report type?
items.explanation: TBL 2-3-12 defines RA as resolution advisory, specifically a pilot-reported TCAS event.

items.question_text: Using FIG 3-9-2, two Category II aircraft are involved and suitable landmarks allow distance determination. The preceding departure is airborne but has not crossed the runway end. What minimum distance must exist before the following aircraft starts takeoff roll?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-2 supports the landmark-distance alternative. When either the succeeding aircraft or both aircraft are Category II, the required distance is 4,500 feet.

choices.text: No distance minimum applies once the preceding aircraft is airborne.

items.question_text: Using FIG 3-9-2, a Category III aircraft is one of the aircraft in the same-runway departure pair. If the preceding aircraft is only airborne and has not crossed the runway end, what landmark-distance minimum applies?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-2 requires 6,000 feet when either aircraft is Category III.

items.question_text: FIG 3-9-3 is the relevant same-runway case. A departure is waiting behind a preceding arrival. What condition must exist before the departure begins takeoff roll?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-3 corresponds to the condition that the preceding landing aircraft is clear of the runway.

choices.text: The preceding arrival has reported the runway in sight.
The preceding arrival is clear of the runway.
The preceding arrival has crossed the threshold.
The departure has been advised of traffic.

items.question_text: Under FIG 3-9-4, a Category G aircraft will depart behind a Category A aircraft from the same runway. Which wake interval applies?
items.explanation: For same-runway or parallel-runway departures separated by less than 2,500 feet, FIG 3-9-4 applies 3 minutes to Category B through I aircraft behind Category A.

choices.text: No wake interval applies because the follower is not Category I.

items.question_text: FIG 3-9-5 and FIG 3-9-6 cover a Category I aircraft departing behind a Category E aircraft. What is required when both use the same runway or parallel runways less than 700 feet apart?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-5 and FIG 3-9-6 support the 2-minute requirement for Category I behind Category E on the same runway or a parallel runway separated by less than 700 feet.

choices.text: No interval if the Category E aircraft is already airborne.

items.question_text: FIG 3-9-7 applies to parallel runways separated by 700 feet or more. A Category I aircraft will depart behind a Category E aircraft and the projected flight paths cross. What interval is required?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-7 extends the Category I-behind-Category E 2-minute requirement to parallel runways separated by 700 feet or more when projected flight paths cross.

choices.text: No interval because the runways are separated by at least 700 feet.

items.question_text: Using FIG 3-9-8, parallel runways are separated by 2,500 feet or more and projected flight paths cross. A Category I aircraft will follow a Category C aircraft. Does the listed wake interval apply?
items.explanation: FIG 3-9-8 lists 2 minutes for Category E through I aircraft behind Category C. A Category I follower is not in that listed follower group.

choices.text: Yes, 2 minutes applies to every follower behind Category C.
Yes, but only if the runways are less than 700 feet apart.
No; the Category C row in this condition applies to Category E through I followers.
No; projected crossing paths eliminate wake requirements.

items.question_text: In the FIG 4-8-1 type case, an aircraft on an unpublished route is already established on a published route or approach segment at an appropriate altitude. What may ATC do?
items.explanation: FIG 4-8-1 illustrates that an approach clearance may be issued after the aircraft is established on a published route or instrument approach segment.

choices.text: Withhold all approach clearances until the aircraft reaches the FAF.
Clear direct to the FAF even when it is not an IAF.
Issue only a visual approach clearance.

items.question_text: In the FIG 4-8-2 type case, an aircraft on an unpublished route is cleared direct to a fix but is not yet established on a published segment. What must the approach clearance include?
items.explanation: FIG 4-8-2 supports assigning an altitude to maintain until the aircraft is established on a published route or instrument approach procedure segment.

choices.text: Only the approach name; altitude is no longer relevant.
A heading to join final, but no altitude restriction.
An altitude to maintain until established on the published segment.
A requirement to cancel IFR before the approach.

items.question_text: FIG 4-8-3 shows a conventional approach with a procedure turn or hold-in-lieu available. If ATC does not want the pilot to execute that maneuver and the intercept angle is 90 degrees or less, what must ATC say?
items.explanation: For the FIG 4-8-3 case, ATC must instruct the pilot to conduct a straight-in approach when the procedure-turn or hold-in-lieu path is depicted and ATC does not want it flown.

choices.text: Say only "cleared approach"; the straight-in intent is implied.
Cancel the procedure turn from the published procedure.

items.question_text: FIG 4-8-4 involves RNAV approach clearance to an IAF/IF. An aircraft would intercept the IF/IAF at more than 90 degrees and ATC does not allow the hold-in-lieu procedure. Is direct-to clearance to that fix appropriate?
items.explanation: FIG 4-8-4 illustrates that a direct-to IAF/IF clearance at greater than 90 degrees is not appropriate unless the aircraft is allowed to execute the hold-in-lieu or applicable procedure.

choices.text: Yes, because RNAV equipment removes all intercept-angle limits.
No, unless the aircraft is allowed to execute the hold-in-lieu or otherwise meets the procedure conditions.
Yes, if the aircraft is at or above the MVA.
No, because RNAV approaches cannot be cleared from unpublished routes.

items.question_text: FIG 4-8-5 depicts RF and TF legs. What restriction applies to an RNAV approach containing an RF leg?
items.explanation: FIG 4-8-5 supports the RF-leg rule: do not clear aircraft direct to any waypoint beginning or within an RF leg.

choices.text: ATC may clear direct to any waypoint within an RF leg if the pilot is RNAV equipped.
ATC may clear direct only to the midpoint of an RF leg.
ATC must avoid assigning any RNAV approach with RF legs.
ATC must not clear direct to a waypoint beginning or within an RF leg.

items.question_text: FIG 4-8-6 and FIG 4-8-7 show TAA-supported approaches. An aircraft is inside the TAA lateral boundary but below the TAA minimum altitude. What should happen before or with the approach clearance?
items.explanation: For FIG 4-8-6 and FIG 4-8-7, being inside the TAA boundary is not enough if the aircraft is below the TAA minimum altitude. It must be assigned an altitude until established or climbed to the TAA altitude.

choices.text: Issue the approach clearance with no altitude restriction because the aircraft is inside the TAA boundary.
Assign an altitude until established on a published segment, or climb the aircraft to the TAA altitude.
Clear direct to the FAF to enter the TAA.
Use only a visual approach clearance.

items.question_text: Using TBL 5-5-1, what directly-behind radar wake spacing is required for a Category G follower behind a Category A leader?
items.explanation: TBL 5-5-1 gives 8 NM for a Category G follower directly behind a Category A leader.

items.question_text: Using TBL 5-5-1, a Category I follower is directly behind a Category B leader. What wake spacing applies?
items.explanation: TBL 5-5-1 gives 5 NM for a Category I follower directly behind a Category B leader.

choices.text: No wake spacing applies to Category I followers.

items.question_text: Using TBL 5-5-2, what on-approach wake spacing applies for a Category I follower behind a Category B leader to the same runway?
items.explanation: TBL 5-5-2 gives 6 NM for a Category I follower behind a Category B leader on approach to the same runway.

items.question_text: A terminal data block displays NOWGT. Under 5-5-4, what separation must be provided around that aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 5-5-4 requires 10 miles behind the preceding aircraft and 10 miles to the succeeding aircraft when NOWGT is displayed. This rule sits immediately after TBL 5-5-2.

choices.text: Use the Category I row in TBL 5-5-2.
Provide 10 miles behind the preceding aircraft and 10 miles to the succeeding aircraft.
Provide 2.5 NM if both aircraft are within 10 NM of the runway.

items.question_text: FIG 6-2-1 applies when successive departures will fly courses diverging by 45 degrees or more immediately after takeoff. What initial separation minimum is used until the courses diverge?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-1 corresponds to the immediate-after-takeoff case: 1 minute until the courses diverge.

items.question_text: FIG 6-2-2 applies when courses will diverge within 5 minutes after takeoff. What minimum is required until divergence?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-2 corresponds to divergence within 5 minutes after takeoff: use 2 minutes until courses diverge.

items.question_text: FIG 6-2-3 applies when aircraft will fly diverging courses within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff. What minimum is used until courses diverge?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-3 corresponds to the within-13-miles DME/ATD case: 3 miles until courses diverge.

items.question_text: Using FIG 6-2-4, two aircraft depart in the same direction from different parallel runways. What runway-centerline condition permits simultaneous takeoffs when the courses diverge immediately after takeoff?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-4 supports simultaneous takeoffs from parallel runways when the centerlines are separated by at least 3,500 feet and the aircraft fly diverging courses immediately after takeoff.

choices.text: Any spacing, if both aircraft are radar identified.

items.question_text: FIG 6-2-5 covers simultaneous takeoffs from nonintersecting diverging runways. If the runways diverge by 15 to 29 degrees inclusive, what centerline spacing is required at and beyond the takeoff points?
items.explanation: For FIG 6-2-5, nonintersecting runways diverging by 15 to 29 degrees inclusive require at least 2,000 feet between centerlines at and beyond the takeoff points.

items.question_text: Under the FIG 6-2-5 nonintersecting-runway case, the runways diverge by less than 15 degrees. What centerline spacing is required for simultaneous takeoffs?
items.explanation: When nonintersecting runways diverge by less than 15 degrees, FIG 6-2-5 requires at least 3,500 feet between centerlines at and beyond the takeoff points.

choices.text: No simultaneous takeoffs are allowed below 15 degrees.

items.question_text: FIG 6-2-7 applies to intersecting runways diverging by 15 to 29 degrees inclusive. What must happen before the succeeding aircraft is authorized for takeoff?
items.explanation: For the FIG 6-2-7 case, the preceding aircraft must pass the runway intersection and have commenced a turn.

choices.text: The preceding aircraft must only be airborne.
The preceding aircraft must pass the runway intersection and commence a turn.
The succeeding aircraft may roll once traffic information is issued.
The runways must be separated by at least 3,500 feet.

items.question_text: In TBL 14-2-1, an uplink message element has response attribute W/U. Which response set is operationally expected?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-1 identifies W/U as requiring a response, with valid responses including WILCO, UNABLE, STANDBY, and defined authority/error responses.

choices.text: WILCO, UNABLE, STANDBY, and defined authority/error responses.
No response unless logical acknowledgement is required.

items.question_text: Using TBL 14-2-6, which CPDLC altitude uplink message content tells the flight crew to maintain a vertical block rather than one altitude?
items.explanation: TBL 14-2-6 lists UM30 as MAINTAIN BLOCK (altitude) TO (altitude), an instruction to maintain a specified vertical range.

items.question_text: TBL 14-3-25 lists UM176. What is the operational meaning of this additional uplink message?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-25 defines UM176 as MAINTAIN OWN SEPARATION AND VMC: the pilot is responsible for separation from other traffic and for maintaining VMC.

choices.text: Maintain own separation and visual meteorological conditions.
Report established on the final approach course.

items.question_text: In TBL 14-3-1, what does response attribute N mean for a CPDLC message element?
items.explanation: TBL 14-3-1 defines N as no response required unless logical acknowledgement is required.

choices.text: No response required unless logical acknowledgement is required.
Any CPDLC downlink message is required.
Only NOT CURRENT DATA AUTHORITY is valid.
